sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 51, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 50 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
RIGHT didn't change anything (board_changed=False). The squares are already at the rightmost position they can go. Let me reconsider.
Initial state: squares at rows 49-52, cols 19-22 (left) and 39-42 (right)
Current state: squares at rows 44-47, cols 24-27 (left) and 34-37 (right)
Wait, the boundaries show:
- Initial: [[49, 19], [49, 23], [53, 23], [53, 19]] - this is rows 49-52, cols 19-22 (4x4=16, but pixels=25 so 5x5)
- Current: [[44, 24], [44, 28], [48, 28], [48, 24]] - rows 44-47, cols 24-27
Hmm, the boundary corners don't match 5x5. Let me think about this differently. The boundary is clockwise outer-perimeter corner points. [[44, 24], [44, 28], [48, 28], [48, 24]] describes a rectangle from row 44 to 48 and col 24 to 28. That's 5 rows (44,45,46,47,48) and 5 cols (24,25,26,27,28) = 25 pixels. OK so it IS 5x5.
So the squares moved:
- Left: from (49,19) to (44,24) = up 5, right 5
- Right: from (49,39) to (44,34) = up 5, left 5
The squares moved toward each other horizontally and both moved up. They're now at the same vertical level.
RIGHT didn't move them further right. So they're at the rightmost position. Let me try LEFT to see if they can move apart again.
Actually wait - the squares are at cols 24-28 and 34-38. There's a gap of 5 cols (29-33) between them. If RIGHT moves them together and they can't move further right, maybe LEFT moves them apart.
But I already know LEFT moves them apart by 5 cols. The question is: what's the goal?
Let me think about this differently. The initial B count was 1453, now it's lower. Let me check the current B count and see if the container has changed.
Actually, I realize I should focus on understanding the game mechanics better. Let me:
1. Check if the container structure has changed (compare initial to current)
2. Try moving squares to different positions to see if there are "target" spots
3. Look for any pattern in the container that suggests where squares should go
Let me try a systematic exploration: move the squares to all possible positions and see if any position triggers a special effect or level completion.
But first, let me understand the movement better. The squares move in steps of 5 pixels. Let me figure out the bounds.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# RIGHT didn't move squares. Let me understand movement bounds and explore.
# Initial: left at (49,19), right at (49,39)
# Current: left at (44,24), right at (44,34)
# Squares are 5x5.

# Let me check current B count to see if container changed
seg = current_frame.segmentation
b_objects = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'B']
print("B objects:")
for n in b_objects:
    print(f"  pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary corners: {n['boundary'][:3]}...")

# Try LEFT to move squares apart
action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Try LEFT again
action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter 2nd LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try moving to initial position and pressing SPACE
# Initial was rows 49, cols 19 and 39
# Current after 2 LEFT should be rows 44, cols 14 and 29
# Need to go DOWN to get to row 49
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter DOWN:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Press SPACE at this position
action(['SPACE'])
print("SPACE result:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'), last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Only affects HUD bars", "LEFT": "Moves squares apart horizontally by 5 cols", "RIGHT": "Moves squares together horizontally by 5 cols", "UP": "Moves squares up by 5 rows", "DOWN": "Moves squares down by 5 rows"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black_containers": "Container with two chambers", "hud_bars": "Top/bottom timer bars"}, "hud_map": {"top_border": "Timer bar", "bottom_border": "Timer bar"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Two 5x5 sky blue squares in black container. Initial: rows 49, cols 19/39. Current: rows 44, cols 24/34. RIGHT blocked at current position.", "goal_model": "Unknown - exploring movement bounds and SPACE effects at different positions.", "recent_findings": ["RIGHT blocked - squares at rightmost position", "Initial B=1453"], "open_questions": ["What triggers level completion?", "Are there target positions?"], "current_plan": "Explore all positions systematically, test SPACE at each.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE brute force", "RIGHT at rightmost position"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
